---
title: " Presentation Introduction"
format: html
---

## What is the Stroop Effect?

- The Stroop effect refers to the cognitive interference that occurs when the brain processes two conflicting pieces of information at the same time

- First documented by J. R. Stroop (1935), it is one of the most studied phenomena in cognitive psychology 

- The Stroop task is widely used to study how the brain manages attention, particularly our ability to inhibit our automatic responses in favour of slower, more deliberate ones

## The Stroop Task

- Participants are shown color words printed in different colors and are asked to name the color of the word, ignoring the word itself 

- Two conditions:
  - Congruent- where the word and ink colour match (e.g. the word "RED" in red ink)
  - Incongruent- where the word and ink colour conflict (e.g. the word "RED" in blue ink)

- Measures reaction time and accuracy as indicators of cognitive interference

## Key Findings

- Stroop (1935) found that participants were slower and less accurate when naming colors on incongruent trials compared to congruent ones 

- This effect has been replicated across hundreds of studies, making it one of the most reliable findings in cognitive psychology

- Repeated practice can reduce interference over time, but it never fully disappears

## Theoretical Explanations

**Relative Speed of Processing:**

- Words are read faster than colors are named, so the word meaning always reaches the response stage first and interferes with color naming 

**Automaticity:**

- Reading is so deeply practiced that it happens automatically. Our brains process the word meaning even when we are trying to ignore it, which interferes with the slower, deliberate process of color naming

## The Present Study

Our study aimed to replicate the Stroop effect in a classroom sample using a computerized task

Hypothesis: Participants will show slower reaction times and lower accuracy on incongruent trials compared to congruent trials 





References:
MacLeod, C. M. (1991). Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review. Psychological Bulletin, 109(2), 163–203. https://doi.org/10.1037/0033-2909.109.2.163

Stroop, J. R. (1935). Studies of interference in serial verbal reactions. Journal of Experimental Psychology, 18(6), 643–662. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0054651
